icd_category: M32.14 — Glomerular disease in systemic lupus erythematosus
one_liner: A 30-year-old African American woman with known SLE presents with facial edema, dark urine, and new hypertension, found to have nephritic-nephrotic overlap syndrome with low complement levels.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was diagnosed with SLE 3 years ago, managed with hydroxychloroquine. She has been mostly stable but over the past 3 weeks has developed progressive facial and lower extremity swelling, dark (cola-colored) urine, and foamy urine. She reports fatigue, low-grade fevers, a new malar rash, and bilateral joint pain in her hands and wrists. She missed her last 2 rheumatology appointments and has been inconsistent with hydroxychloroquine. She denies flank pain, cough, or chest pain.
stage2.pmh: Systemic lupus erythematosus (3 years; history of malar rash, arthritis, positive ANA/anti-dsDNA), iron deficiency anemia.
stage2.family_history: Mother has SLE. Maternal aunt has rheumatoid arthritis.
stage2.social_history: Elementary school teacher. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. No illicit drug use. Single, no children.
stage2.physical_exam: Young woman with visible facial puffiness and malar rash (butterfly distribution, sparing nasolabial folds). Periorbital edema. Oral ulcers on the palate. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: **decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases**. Abdomen: **mild distension, shifting dullness, no tenderness or organomegaly**. Extremities: **2+ bilateral pitting edema to the knees**; **swelling and tenderness of MCP and PIP joints bilaterally without deformity**. Skin: **malar rash, no discoid lesions or vasculitic ulcers**.

lab_results.result: 3+ blood, 3+ protein, >50 RBCs/hpf (many dysmorphic), RBC casts, WBC casts, granular casts
lab_results.clinical_significance: Active nephritic sediment with RBC casts plus heavy proteinuria = nephritic-nephrotic overlap; classic for proliferative this condition
Nephrotic-range proteinuria; combined with nephritic features suggests Class IV (or III/IV) this condition
Elevated, indicating impaired renal function from active nephritis
Severely depressed; complement consumption is a hallmark of active this condition
Low C4 supports active lupus with classic complement pathway activation
Markedly elevated; anti-dsDNA titers correlate with this condition activity
High-titer ANA consistent with active SLE
Pancytopenia common in active SLE; anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia
Highly specific for SLE (though not sensitive); confirms SLE diagnosis

imaging_results.result: Bilateral kidneys mildly enlarged (right 12.8 cm, left 13.0 cm) with increased cortical echogenicity. No hydronephrosis or masses.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Enlarged echogenic kidneys consistent with active nephritis

imaging_results.result: Light microscopy: diffuse endocapillary and extracapillary proliferation involving >50% of glomeruli, with wire-loop deposits and cellular crescents in 20% of glomeruli. Immunofluorescence: 'full house' pattern — IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C4, and C1q deposition. Electron microscopy: subendothelial, subepithelial, and mesangial electron-dense deposits. ISN/RPS Class IV-G (A).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Class IV this condition this condition is the most severe and common form of this condition; 'full house' IF pattern is virtually pathognomonic for this condition

debrief.classic_presentation: A young woman (especially African American or Hispanic) with active SLE (rash, arthritis, cytopenias) presenting with hypertension, edema, hematuria, and proteinuria (nephritic-nephrotic overlap). Labs show elevated anti-dsDNA, low complement, and active urinary sediment. Biopsy shows diffuse proliferative GN with 'full house' IF.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The ISN/RPS classification of lupus nephritis drives treatment: Class I/II (minimal/mesangial) — supportive care. Class III (focal proliferative) — immunosuppression if active lesions. Class IV (diffuse proliferative) — aggressive immunosuppression (most common and severe class). Class V (membranous) — immunosuppression if nephrotic-range proteinuria. Class VI (sclerosing) — >90% sclerosed, no active treatment. 'Full house' immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C4, C1q) is virtually pathognomonic for lupus nephritis. Anti-dsDNA titers and complement levels can be used to monitor disease activity, but treatment changes should be based on clinical assessment, not serologies alone.
debrief.pathophysiology: Lupus nephritis results from deposition of immune complexes (primarily anti-dsDNA/DNA complexes) in the glomeruli. In Class IV, immune complexes deposit predominantly in the subendothelial space, activating complement via the classical pathway (low C3, C4, C1q) and triggering neutrophil infiltration, endocapillary proliferation, and crescent formation. The 'full house' pattern on immunofluorescence reflects the polyclonal nature of the immune response in SLE. Subendothelial deposits produce 'wire-loop' lesions on light microscopy. The nephritic-nephrotic overlap pattern occurs because proliferative inflammation damages the glomerular filtration barrier (proteinuria) while inflammatory cell infiltration reduces GFR (nephritic features).
debrief.treatment_overview: INDUCTION therapy (6 months): mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 2-3 g/day or IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus or NIH protocol), PLUS glucocorticoids (IV pulse methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisone taper). Voclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor) may be added to MMF per the AURORA trial. Belimumab (anti-BAFF) added to standard therapy reduces renal flares per the BLISS-LN trial. MAINTENANCE therapy: MMF (1-2 g/day) or azathioprine, with low-dose prednisone. Hydroxychloroquine should be continued indefinitely (reduces flares and improves survival). ACE inhibitor/ARB for proteinuria and BP control. Duration: minimum 3-5 years of maintenance; monitor anti-dsDNA, complement, proteinuria, and creatinine for relapse.
high_yield_pearls: Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis is the most common and most severe class, occurring in ~40% of patients with lupus nephritis.
'Full house' immunofluorescence (IgG + IgA + IgM + C3 + C4 + C1q) is virtually pathognomonic for lupus nephritis; no other glomerular disease shows this pattern.
Low C3/C4 and elevated anti-dsDNA are the best serologic markers for active lupus nephritis; complement levels often normalize with successful treatment.
The AURORA trial demonstrated that adding voclosporin to MMF and steroids significantly improved complete renal response compared to MMF and steroids alone.
Hydroxychloroquine should be continued in ALL lupus patients — it reduces renal flares, thromboembolic events, and improves overall survival.
sources: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Lupus Nephritis
Rovin BH et al. Efficacy and safety of voclosporin versus placebo for lupus nephritis (AURORA 1). Lancet. 2021;397(10289):2070-2080.
UpToDate: Lupus nephritis — diagnosis, classification, and treatment (2024)

after_stage2_more_likely: Lupus nephritis — known SLE with active flare (malar rash, arthritis, oral ulcers), new hypertension, edema, dark urine
IgA nephropathy — hematuria and proteinuria, but SLE features make lupus nephritis more likely
after_stage2_less_likely: Post-infectious GN — no recent streptococcal infection; active SLE features explain findings
Anti-GBM disease — no hemoptysis; young African American woman with SLE is classic for lupus nephritis
after_labs_top3: Lupus nephritis Class IV — nephritic-nephrotic overlap, severely low C3/C4, high anti-dsDNA, 'full house' IF on biopsy, diffuse proliferative GN
Membranoproliferative GN — can also have low complement and nephritic-nephrotic overlap, but 'full house' IF is specific for lupus
Post-infectious GN — low C3 can occur, but C4 would be normal (alternate pathway); no infectious prodrome
